currentQuizCards.name: Nine of Swords
currentQuizCards.upright: A figure sits up in bed, head in hands, nine swords on the wall above them. This is the card of 3am thinking: the anxiety that is worst in the dark, the fears that crowd in when defences are down, the mental anguish that outpaces its actual cause. In a reading the Nine of Swords names the suffering without endorsing the story producing it — and asks whether the worst fears are actually true.
currentQuizCards.reversed: Moving through the darkest period, or anxiety so deep it cannot be named.
currentQuizCards.relational: Next to The Moon: fear and illusion compounding each other. Next to The Star: the night ending. Next to The Four of Swords: rest as the antidote to rumination.

scenario.love: Catastrophic thinking about a relationship — the anxiety that runs ahead of reality.
scenario.work: Worry about a situation that may be far less dire than it feels at night.
scenario.self: Which of the thoughts at 3am are worth examining in daylight, and which are just fear?

currentQuizCards.name: Five of Cups
currentQuizCards.upright: A cloaked figure stands before three spilled cups, staring down. Behind them, two cups remain upright, and a bridge leads to a house in the distance. The Five of Cups is the card of grief and regret — real loss that deserves acknowledgement. But the two standing cups and the bridge are always there. In a reading it asks: how long will you stand before the spilled cups before turning around?
currentQuizCards.reversed: Beginning to move through grief, acceptance arriving, or refusing to acknowledge a genuine loss.
currentQuizCards.relational: Next to Death: profound and necessary loss. Next to The Star: recovery is genuinely possible. Next to The Three of Cups: what was lost was connection.

scenario.love: Grief after a relationship ending, or dwelling on what went wrong rather than what remains.
scenario.work: Mourning a lost opportunity or role rather than redirecting toward what is still available.
scenario.self: What would you see if you turned away from what was lost and looked at what remains?

currentQuizCards.name: Seven of Pentacles
currentQuizCards.upright: A farmer leans on his hoe, surveying the pentacles growing on the vine before him. He has worked. Now he waits. The Seven of Pentacles speaks to the patience of long-term investment — the kind of work whose results cannot be rushed. In a reading it often asks whether the querent is willing to trust the process, or whether they are looking for a quicker return than the situation allows.
currentQuizCards.reversed: Impatience, poor investment choices, or work that is not yielding results because it is genuinely the wrong approach.
currentQuizCards.relational: Next to The Star: trusting the slow work of recovery. Next to The Three of Pentacles: patient skill building toward mastery. Next to The Wheel: timing mattering as much as effort.

scenario.love: A relationship that requires sustained investment before it bears fruit.
scenario.work: A long-term project, skill development, or business that is not yet paying off but will.
scenario.self: What are you tending that you keep pulling up to check the roots?

currentQuizCards.name: Six of Swords
currentQuizCards.upright: A ferryman poles a boat carrying a woman and child, six swords lodged in the bow, toward calmer water. The Six of Swords is passage — moving away from turbulence toward something more still. It is not triumphant; the swords are still in the boat, and the passengers are subdued. But movement is happening, and the water ahead is calmer. In a reading it often signals that the worst is behind you.
currentQuizCards.reversed: Resistance to necessary transition, or forced departure from somewhere that still felt like home.
currentQuizCards.relational: Next to Death: necessary departure from what is over. Next to The Star: the destination that awaits. Next to The Eight of Cups: choosing to move rather than being made to.

scenario.love: Moving on from a relationship — not in denial, but with quiet resolution.
scenario.work: Transitioning out of a role, organisation, or way of working.
scenario.self: What are you moving away from, and are you allowing yourself to face forward?

currentQuizCards.name: Two of Pentacles
currentQuizCards.upright: A figure juggles two pentacles connected by an infinity loop, ships rising and falling on waves behind him. The Two of Pentacles speaks to the ongoing work of keeping multiple things in the air — financial priorities, time, commitments. It is not crisis; the juggling is working. But it asks how long this can continue, and what would happen if one thing became the priority.
currentQuizCards.reversed: Overwhelm, dropping the balls, poor financial decisions under pressure.
currentQuizCards.relational: Next to The Ten of Wands: the juggling becoming unsustainable. Next to Temperance: balance achieved with grace. Next to The Chariot: managing competing demands with real willpower.

scenario.love: A relationship competing with other commitments — time and energy being stretched.
scenario.work: Managing multiple projects or income streams simultaneously.
scenario.self: What would you stop juggling if you admitted it was too much?

currentQuizCards.name: Four of Wands
currentQuizCards.upright: Two figures dance beneath a garland strung between four wands, a castle behind them, people celebrating. The Four of Wands marks a genuine milestone — a homecoming, a wedding, a completion worth celebrating publicly. It speaks to the joy of arrival, of belonging, of a structure that holds. In a reading it is nearly always welcome.
currentQuizCards.reversed: A celebration that feels hollow, instability beneath a happy surface, or a milestone that doesn't feel earned.
currentQuizCards.relational: Next to The Ten of Cups: deep and lasting belonging. Next to The Three of Cups: a community gathering. Next to The Tower: what is being celebrated is more fragile than it appears.

scenario.love: A committed relationship reaching a milestone — engagement, moving in, a genuine declaration.
scenario.work: A project completion or organisational achievement worth marking.
scenario.self: What milestone in your own life are you not allowing yourself to celebrate?

spread.name: Three Card
spread.description: Past, Present, Future — the spine of any reading.

card.name: Three of Cups
card.upright: Three women dance together, cups raised, surrounded by flowers and fruit. The Three of Cups is unambiguously joyful — a moment of celebration, friendship, and shared abundance. It speaks to chosen community, to the people who show up, to the pleasure of being genuinely known and enjoyed. In a reading it often signals good news, reunion, or a moment worth marking.
card.reversed: Overindulgence, a celebration that has become hollow, or a social group with a dark undercurrent. Sometimes gossip or exclusion.
card.relational: Next to The Sun: a genuinely happy period. Next to The Moon: celebration concealing something. Next to The Five of Cups: joy that recently followed loss.

scenario.love: A relationship that has real joy and friendship at its core — not just intensity.
scenario.work: A team celebrating genuine success, or a creative collaboration firing on all cylinders.
scenario.self: When did you last let yourself be genuinely celebrated? Or celebrate someone else?

card.name: Four of Cups
card.upright: A young man sits under a tree, arms crossed, staring at three cups before him. A fourth cup is offered from a cloud — he doesn't even look at it. The Four of Cups speaks to the kind of dissatisfaction that misses what is being offered, whether from boredom, self-absorption, or genuine need for inner retreat. Sometimes the withdrawal is appropriate; sometimes it is blindness.
card.reversed: Emerging from withdrawal, finally noticing what's available, or a period of useful inwardness coming to an end.
card.relational: Next to The Hermit: deliberate withdrawal. Next to The Star: depression lifting. Next to The Ace of Cups: an offer being missed.

scenario.love: Emotional unavailability — your own or a partner's. Something is being offered that isn't being received.
scenario.work: Boredom with a role that could be interesting, or missing an opportunity because attention is elsewhere.
scenario.self: What are you refusing to see that is right in front of you?

card.name: The Hanged Man
card.upright: He hangs by one foot, at peace. He chose this. The Hanged Man is voluntarily suspended — not trapped but paused, deliberately, to see the world from a different angle. In readings he calls for surrender rather than struggle, for the kind of waiting that is actually doing something profound. What you cannot see from your current position becomes visible when you let go and invert.
card.reversed: Needless martyrdom, or suspension becoming stagnation. Refusing to make a necessary sacrifice. Waiting that has become avoidance.
card.relational: Next to The Wheel: a pause before the next turn. Next to The Star: stillness that will lead to healing. Next to The Eight of Swords: paralysis dressed up as patience.

scenario.love: A relationship put on hold — or the moment of surrendering the need to control the outcome.
scenario.work: A project requires waiting. Push harder and you lose. Let it breathe.
scenario.self: What are you refusing to release your grip on?

currentReading.querrant: A client has recently been made redundant after 12 years at the same company. They feel lost.

steps.prompt: You look at the spread. The Hierophant sits in the Past position. Before addressing the redundancy directly, what does this card tell you about how they experienced that 12-year period?

options.text: They valued the structure and identity that the institution gave them — perhaps too much.
options.chosen_response: Yes. The Hierophant in the past position often signals that the querent found significant meaning in belonging to a structure. 12 years at one company, especially if that company had strong culture or hierarchy, can become an identity. The first thing worth acknowledging is that what was lost wasn't just a job.

options.text: They were probably too conventional in how they worked — never challenged the rules.
options.chosen_response: That's a possible reading, but it's evaluative rather than curious. The Hierophant in past position more usefully asks: what did that structure mean to them? Judgement about whether they should have challenged it comes later, if at all.

options.text: Religious or cultural values shaped their career choices.
options.chosen_response: Possible, but you're narrowing too fast. The Hierophant's institutional dimension is broader than religion. The more useful question at this stage is: how much of their identity was tied to belonging to that particular institution?

steps._chosen_response: Yes. The Hierophant in the past position often signals that the querent found significant meaning in belonging to a structure. 12 years at one company, especially if that company had strong culture or hierarchy, can become an identity. The first thing worth acknowledging is that what was lost wasn't just a job.

steps.prompt: Death sits in the Present. How do you introduce this card to someone who is already frightened?

options.text: Immediately reassure them that Death never means actual death, then explain the transformation meaning.
options.chosen_response: The reassurance impulse is understandable, but leading with it actually increases anxiety — it signals there's something to be scared of. A better approach: name what is true. Something has ended. That is real, and it matters. Then open toward what becomes possible when something ends.

options.text: Acknowledge that something genuinely has ended, and that grief is the right response — then ask what they are still trying to hold onto.
options.chosen_response: Exactly. Death in the present isn't a threat — it's a description. Something has ended. The grief is real and worth honouring. But the productive question for a reading is: what are they still trying to sustain that has already gone? That's where the actual work lives.

options.text: Focus on transformation — this is a new beginning in disguise.
options.chosen_response: You're jumping to the hopeful frame too quickly. Transformation is part of the card's meaning, but if you go straight there you skip the acknowledgement of loss that the querent actually needs to hear first. The sequence matters.

steps._chosen_response: You're jumping to the hopeful frame too quickly. Transformation is part of the card's meaning, but if you go straight there you skip the acknowledgement of loss that the querent actually needs to hear first. The sequence matters.

steps.prompt: The Star closes the spread in the Future position. After what you've said about the Hierophant and Death, how do you read this?

options.text: Everything will be fine — the Star is a positive card and the future looks good.
options.chosen_response: Too easy. The Star is genuinely hopeful but it isn't a promise of outcome — it's a quality of possibility. Given that the Past shows deep institutional identity and the Present shows real loss, the Star in the future position is asking: can you let yourself hope for something that is genuinely yours, rather than something given to you by an institution?

options.text: Recovery and renewal are available — but the sequence suggests it requires genuinely releasing what's gone, not just waiting it out.
options.chosen_response: Good. The narrative arc across these three cards is: deep institutional belonging → real ending → the possibility of something more authentically one's own. The Star is available, but it follows Death. You can't rush the middle card.

options.text: They should look for work in a field that feels more meaningful and personally chosen.
options.chosen_response: That might be true but you've shifted from reading the cards to giving career advice. The Star speaks to the inner state that makes what comes next possible — renewed hope, trust in the world, a less defended openness. Stay with that.

currentQuizFeedback.good: Well spotted. Red Rose — Desire, passion, and the material — the Rose of Venus. What the heart wants in this world. At this level it also appears on: Two of Wands.
currentQuizFeedback.bad: Look again — the card carries this one: Red Rose — Desire, passion, and the material — the Rose of Venus. What the heart wants in this world. At this level it also appears on: Two of Wands.

profile.title: The Initiated Reader
profile.summary: Your instincts are already moving beyond surface symbolism. You understand that position shapes meaning, that cards in combination create narrative, and that the useful question is rarely what a card "means" in isolation.
profile.startPoint: Begin directly with spread practice. The Minor Arcana will be your focus — you don't need the Major Arcana foundations.
